1→---
     2→title: Fleet Maintenance Terminology Glossary
     3→category: domain
     4→difficulty: foundational
     5→tags: [glossary, terminology, definitions, vocabulary]
     6→related_docs: [01-problem-definition, 03-industry-standards]
     7→summary: Comprehensive glossary of fleet maintenance scheduling terms, acronyms, and domain-specific vocabulary.
     8→version: 1.0
     9→last_updated: 2025-12-07
    10→---
    11→
    12→# Fleet Maintenance Terminology Glossary
    13→
    14→This glossary defines key terms used in fleet maintenance scheduling systems. Terms are organized by category for easier reference. Each term includes a definition and, where applicable, context for how it applies to scheduling optimization.
    15→
    16→## Maintenance Types and Strategies
    17→
    18→### Preventive Maintenance (PM)
    19→
    20→Scheduled maintenance performed at predetermined intervals to prevent failures. Intervals may be based on time (calendar days), usage (mileage, operating hours), or cycles (door openings, starts).
    21→
    22→```typescript
    23→interface PreventiveMaintenance {
    24→  id: string;
    25→  name: string;
    26→  intervalType: 'time' | 'mileage' | 'hours' | 'cycles';
    27→  intervalValue: number;
    28→  toleranceWindow: { before: number; after: number };
    29→  durationHours: number;
    30→  requiredResources: ResourceRequirement[];
    31→}
    32→```
    33→
    34→### Corrective Maintenance (CM)
    35→
    36→Unplanned maintenance performed after a failure or defect is detected. Not directly scheduled but affects capacity available for preventive maintenance.
    37→
    38→### Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)
    39→
    40→Maintenance triggered by monitored asset condition rather than fixed intervals. Requires sensors and data analysis to detect degradation before failure.
    41→
    42→### Predictive Maintenance (PdM)
    43→
    44→Uses data analytics and machine learning to predict when maintenance will be needed. Enables dynamic scheduling based on predicted remaining useful life.
    45→
    46→### Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)
    47→
    48→A systematic approach to determining what maintenance is required to ensure assets continue to fulfill their intended functions. Focuses on failure modes and consequences.
    49→
    50→## Usage and Measurement Terms
    51→
    52→### Mileage (Odometer Reading)
    53→
    54→Cumulative distance traveled by an asset. Primary trigger for maintenance in transportation fleets. Typically measured in kilometers or miles.
    55→
    56→### Operating Hours
    57→
    58→Cumulative time an asset has been in active operation. Common for stationary equipment, engines, and aircraft.
    59→
    60→### Cycles
    61→
    62→Count of discrete operational events. Examples: flight cycles (takeoff-landing), door opening cycles, engine starts.
    63→
    64→### Remaining Useful Life (RUL)
    65→
    66→Estimated time, mileage, or cycles remaining before maintenance is required. Calculated as interval minus usage since last maintenance.
    67→
    68→```typescript
    69→interface RemainingUsefulLife {
    70→  assetId: string;
    71→  maintenanceType: string;
    72→  currentUsage: number;
    73→  intervalLimit: number;
    74→  remaining: number;
    75→  remainingPercent: number;
    76→  estimatedDueDate: Date;
    77→}
    78→```
    79→
    80→### Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)
    81→
    82→Statistical measure of reliability. Average operating time between failures for a repairable system.
    83→
    84→### Mean Distance Between Failures (MDBF)
    85→
    86→Similar to MTBF but measured in distance. Common in railway and transportation contexts.
    87→
    88→## Scheduling Terms
    89→
    90→### Master Plan
    91→
    92→Long-term maintenance schedule covering months to years. Defines when each major maintenance event will occur. Serves as the baseline for tactical and operational planning.
    93→
    94→### Rolling Horizon
    95→
    96→Planning approach where the schedule extends a fixed period into the future and is regularly updated. As time passes, the horizon "rolls" forward and new periods are added.
    97→
    98→### Planning Window
    99→
   100→The time frame covered by the current schedule. May vary from weeks (operational) to years (strategic).
   101→
   102→### Tolerance Window
   103→
   104→The acceptable range around a maintenance target date. An event scheduled at 50,000 km with ±5,000 km tolerance can occur between 45,000 and 55,000 km.
   105→
   106→### Resource Leveling
   107→
   108→The process of adjusting the schedule to distribute workload evenly across time periods. Reduces peak resource requirements.
   109→
   110→```typescript
   111→interface ResourceLevelingResult {
   112→  originalPeakLoad: number;
   113→  leveledPeakLoad: number;
   114→  reductionPercent: number;
   115→  varianceBeforeLeveling: number;
   116→  varianceAfterLeveling: number;
   117→}
   118→```
   119→
   120→### Drift
   121→
   122→Deviation between planned and actual values. Positive drift means actual exceeds planned; negative means actual is below planned.
   123→
   124→```typescript
   125→interface DriftMetrics {
   126→  plannedMileage: number;
   127→  actualMileage: number;
   128→  absoluteDrift: number;
   129→  relativeDriftPercent: number;
   130→  trend: 'accelerating' | 'stable' | 'decelerating';
   131→}
   132→```
   133→
   134→## Resource Terms
   135→
   136→### Man-Hours
   137→
   138→Unit of work representing one person working for one hour. Used to measure maintenance labor requirements and capacity.
   139→
   140→### Bay (Maintenance Bay)
   141→
   142→Physical space where maintenance is performed. May include equipment like lifts, pits, or specialized tooling. Key capacity constraint.
   143→
   144→### Track (Maintenance Track)
   145→
   146→In railway context, the rail track within a depot where trains undergo maintenance. Equivalent to maintenance bays for road vehicles.
   147→
   148→### Depot
   149→
   150→Facility where assets are stored and maintained. May have multiple maintenance bays and different capability levels.
   151→
   152→### Work Order
   153→
   154→A document or record specifying maintenance work to be performed. Contains task details, required parts, assigned resources, and schedule.
   155→
   156→## Optimization Terms
   157→
   158→### Mixed Integer Programming (MIP)
   159→
   160→Mathematical optimization technique where some variables must take integer values. Used when decisions are discrete (e.g., which day to schedule maintenance).
   161→
   162→### Constraint Programming (CP)
   163→
   164→Optimization approach based on satisfying constraints. Particularly effective for scheduling problems with complex logical constraints.
   165→
   166→### Objective Function
   167→
   168→Mathematical expression defining what to optimize. May minimize (costs, variance) or maximize (availability, throughput).
   169→
   170→### Feasibility
   171→
   172→A schedule is feasible if it satisfies all constraints. An infeasible schedule violates at least one constraint.
   173→
   174→### Optimality
   175→
   176→A schedule is optimal if no other feasible schedule has a better objective value. Solvers may find optimal solutions or prove optimality bounds.
   177→
   178→### Heuristic
   179→
   180→An algorithm that finds good (but not provably optimal) solutions quickly. Used when exact optimization is too slow.
   181→
   182→### Metaheuristic
   183→
   184→A higher-level heuristic that guides other heuristics. Examples include genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, and large neighborhood search.
   185→
   186→## System Integration Terms
   187→
   188→### Enterprise Asset Management (EAM)
   189→
   190→Software platform for managing asset lifecycle, maintenance, and work orders. Major vendors include IBM Maximo and SAP PM.
   191→
   192→### Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS)
   193→
   194→Software for organizing and tracking maintenance activities. Subset of EAM focused on maintenance operations.
   195→
   196→### Application Programming Interface (API)
   197→
   198→Interface allowing software systems to communicate. REST and GraphQL are common API patterns for maintenance systems.
   199→
   200→### Webhook
   201→
   202→A callback mechanism where one system sends real-time notifications to another when events occur. Used for triggering actions on maintenance completion.
   203→
   204→## Industry Standard Terms
   205→
   206→### ISO 55000
   207→
   208→International standard for asset management systems. Defines requirements for establishing, implementing, and improving asset management.
   209→
   210→### EN 13306
   211→
   212→European standard defining maintenance terminology. Provides standardized definitions for maintenance concepts.
   213→
   214→### RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability, Safety)
   215→
   216→Framework for assessing and specifying the dependability of systems. Common in railway and safety-critical industries.
   217→
   218→```typescript
   219→interface RAMSMetrics {
   220→  reliability: number;        // Probability of no failure over period
   221→  availability: number;       // Proportion of time operational
   222→  maintainability: number;    // Ease of restoring after failure
   223→  safetyIntegrityLevel: 1 | 2 | 3 | 4;  // SIL rating
   224→}
   225→```
   226→
   227→### Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)
   228→
   229→Complete cost of an asset over its lifecycle, including acquisition, operation, maintenance, and disposal.
   230→
   231→## Fleet-Specific Terms
   232→
   233→### Train Set / Consist
   234→
   235→A train unit consisting of multiple cars that operate together. May be fixed formation or variable.
   236→
   237→### Headway
   238→
   239→Time interval between consecutive vehicles on a route. Affects how many vehicles can be released for maintenance.
   240→
   241→### Dead Running
   242→
   243→Movement of a vehicle without passengers, typically to/from a depot. Adds to mileage but not revenue.
   244→
   245→### Peak Vehicle Requirement (PVR)
   246→
   247→Maximum number of vehicles needed simultaneously to operate the service schedule.
   248→
   249→## RUC System Terms
   250→
   251→### Recommended Usage Category (RUC)
   252→
   253→Classification system recommending how intensively an asset should be used based on its position relative to the maintenance plan.
   254→
   255→```typescript
   256→type RUCCategory = 'ACCELERATE' | 'NOMINAL' | 'DECELERATE';
   257→
   258→interface RUCRecommendation {
   259→  assetId: string;
   260→  category: RUCCategory;
   261→  currentDrift: number;
   262→  recommendedUsage: string;
   263→  reason: string;
   264→}
   265→```
   266→
   267→### ACCELERATE
   268→
   269→RUC category indicating the asset is under-utilized relative to plan. Recommends assigning to higher-mileage routes.
   270→
   271→### NOMINAL
   272→
   273→RUC category indicating the asset is on-track with the plan. Continue normal assignment.
   274→
   275→### DECELERATE
   276→
   277→RUC category indicating the asset is over-utilized relative to plan. Recommends assigning to lower-mileage routes or reserving as spare.
   278→
   279→## Statistical Terms
   280→
   281→### EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average)
   282→
   283→Statistical technique giving more weight to recent observations. Used for drift detection and trend analysis.
   284→
   285→### CUSUM (Cumulative Sum)
   286→
   287→Control chart method for detecting small persistent shifts in a process. Useful for identifying gradual drift from plan.
   288→
   289→### Variance
   290→
   291→Statistical measure of spread. In resource leveling, lower variance indicates more even workload distribution.
   292→
   293→## Next Steps
   294→
   295→With this vocabulary established:
   296→
   297→- Continue to `03-industry-standards.md` for compliance requirements
   298→- Explore `01-problem-definition.md` for problem context
   299→- Study `02-MATHEMATICAL/01-optimization-theory.md` for mathematical concepts
   300→

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

---
title: Fleet Maintenance Terminology Glossary
category: domain
difficulty: foundational
tags: [glossary, terminology, definitions, vocabulary]
related_docs: [01-problem-definition, 03-industry-standards]
summary: Comprehensive glossary of fleet maintenance scheduling terms, acronyms, and domain-specific vocabulary.
version: 1.0
last_updated: 2025-12-07
---

# Fleet Maintenance Terminology Glossary

This glossary defines key terms used in fleet maintenance scheduling systems. Terms are organized by category for easier reference. Each term includes a definition and, where applicable, context for how it applies to scheduling optimization.

## Maintenance Types and Strategies

### Preventive Maintenance (PM)

Scheduled maintenance performed at predetermined intervals to prevent failures. Intervals may be based on time (calendar days), usage (mileage, operating hours), or cycles (door openings, starts).

```typescript
interface PreventiveMaintenance {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  intervalType: 'time' | 'mileage' | 'hours' | 'cycles';
  intervalValue: number;
  toleranceWindow: { before: number; after: number };
  durationHours: number;
  requiredResources: ResourceRequirement[];
}
```

### Corrective Maintenance (CM)

Unplanned maintenance performed after a failure or defect is detected. Not directly scheduled but affects capacity available for preventive maintenance.

### Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)

Maintenance triggered by monitored asset condition rather than fixed intervals. Requires sensors and data analysis to detect degradation before failure.

### Predictive Maintenance (PdM)

Uses data analytics and machine learning to predict when maintenance will be needed. Enables dynamic scheduling based on predicted remaining useful life.

### Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)

A systematic approach to determining what maintenance is required to ensure assets continue to fulfill their intended functions. Focuses on failure modes and consequences.

## Usage and Measurement Terms

### Mileage (Odometer Reading)

Cumulative distance traveled by an asset. Primary trigger for maintenance in transportation fleets. Typically measured in kilometers or miles.

### Operating Hours

Cumulative time an asset has been in active operation. Common for stationary equipment, engines, and aircraft.

### Cycles

Count of discrete operational events. Examples: flight cycles (takeoff-landing), door opening cycles, engine starts.

### Remaining Useful Life (RUL)

Estimated time, mileage, or cycles remaining before maintenance is required. Calculated as interval minus usage since last maintenance.

```typescript
interface RemainingUsefulLife {
  assetId: string;
  maintenanceType: string;
  currentUsage: number;
  intervalLimit: number;
  remaining: number;
  remainingPercent: number;
  estimatedDueDate: Date;
}
```

### Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

Statistical measure of reliability. Average operating time between failures for a repairable system.

### Mean Distance Between Failures (MDBF)

Similar to MTBF but measured in distance. Common in railway and transportation contexts.

## Scheduling Terms

### Master Plan

Long-term maintenance schedule covering months to years. Defines when each major maintenance event will occur. Serves as the baseline for tactical and operational planning.

### Rolling Horizon

Planning approach where the schedule extends a fixed period into the future and is regularly updated. As time passes, the horizon "rolls" forward and new periods are added.

### Planning Window

The time frame covered by the current schedule. May vary from weeks (operational) to years (strategic).

### Tolerance Window

The acceptable range around a maintenance target date. An event scheduled at 50,000 km with ±5,000 km tolerance can occur between 45,000 and 55,000 km.

### Resource Leveling

The process of adjusting the schedule to distribute workload evenly across time periods. Reduces peak resource requirements.

```typescript
interface ResourceLevelingResult {
  originalPeakLoad: number;
  leveledPeakLoad: number;
  reductionPercent: number;
  varianceBeforeLeveling: number;
  varianceAfterLeveling: number;
}
```

### Drift

Deviation between planned and actual values. Positive drift means actual exceeds planned; negative means actual is below planned.

```typescript
interface DriftMetrics {
  plannedMileage: number;
  actualMileage: number;
  absoluteDrift: number;
  relativeDriftPercent: number;
  trend: 'accelerating' | 'stable' | 'decelerating';
}
```

## Resource Terms

### Man-Hours

Unit of work representing one person working for one hour. Used to measure maintenance labor requirements and capacity.

### Bay (Maintenance Bay)

Physical space where maintenance is performed. May include equipment like lifts, pits, or specialized tooling. Key capacity constraint.

### Track (Maintenance Track)

In railway context, the rail track within a depot where trains undergo maintenance. Equivalent to maintenance bays for road vehicles.

### Depot

Facility where assets are stored and maintained. May have multiple maintenance bays and different capability levels.

### Work Order

A document or record specifying maintenance work to be performed. Contains task details, required parts, assigned resources, and schedule.

## Optimization Terms

### Mixed Integer Programming (MIP)

Mathematical optimization technique where some variables must take integer values. Used when decisions are discrete (e.g., which day to schedule maintenance).

### Constraint Programming (CP)

Optimization approach based on satisfying constraints. Particularly effective for scheduling problems with complex logical constraints.

### Objective Function

Mathematical expression defining what to optimize. May minimize (costs, variance) or maximize (availability, throughput).

### Feasibility

A schedule is feasible if it satisfies all constraints. An infeasible schedule violates at least one constraint.

### Optimality

A schedule is optimal if no other feasible schedule has a better objective value. Solvers may find optimal solutions or prove optimality bounds.

### Heuristic

An algorithm that finds good (but not provably optimal) solutions quickly. Used when exact optimization is too slow.

### Metaheuristic

A higher-level heuristic that guides other heuristics. Examples include genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, and large neighborhood search.

## System Integration Terms

### Enterprise Asset Management (EAM)

Software platform for managing asset lifecycle, maintenance, and work orders. Major vendors include IBM Maximo and SAP PM.

### Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS)

Software for organizing and tracking maintenance activities. Subset of EAM focused on maintenance operations.

### Application Programming Interface (API)

Interface allowing software systems to communicate. REST and GraphQL are common API patterns for maintenance systems.

### Webhook

A callback mechanism where one system sends real-time notifications to another when events occur. Used for triggering actions on maintenance completion.

## Industry Standard Terms

### ISO 55000

International standard for asset management systems. Defines requirements for establishing, implementing, and improving asset management.

### EN 13306

European standard defining maintenance terminology. Provides standardized definitions for maintenance concepts.

### RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability, Safety)

Framework for assessing and specifying the dependability of systems. Common in railway and safety-critical industries.

```typescript
interface RAMSMetrics {
  reliability: number;        // Probability of no failure over period
  availability: number;       // Proportion of time operational
  maintainability: number;    // Ease of restoring after failure
  safetyIntegrityLevel: 1 | 2 | 3 | 4;  // SIL rating
}
```

### Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)

Complete cost of an asset over its lifecycle, including acquisition, operation, maintenance, and disposal.

## Fleet-Specific Terms

### Train Set / Consist

A train unit consisting of multiple cars that operate together. May be fixed formation or variable.

### Headway

Time interval between consecutive vehicles on a route. Affects how many vehicles can be released for maintenance.

### Dead Running

Movement of a vehicle without passengers, typically to/from a depot. Adds to mileage but not revenue.

### Peak Vehicle Requirement (PVR)

Maximum number of vehicles needed simultaneously to operate the service schedule.

## RUC System Terms

### Recommended Usage Category (RUC)

Classification system recommending how intensively an asset should be used based on its position relative to the maintenance plan.

```typescript
type RUCCategory = 'ACCELERATE' | 'NOMINAL' | 'DECELERATE';

interface RUCRecommendation {
  assetId: string;
  category: RUCCategory;
  currentDrift: number;
  recommendedUsage: string;
  reason: string;
}
```

### ACCELERATE

RUC category indicating the asset is under-utilized relative to plan. Recommends assigning to higher-mileage routes.

### NOMINAL

RUC category indicating the asset is on-track with the plan. Continue normal assignment.

### DECELERATE

RUC category indicating the asset is over-utilized relative to plan. Recommends assigning to lower-mileage routes or reserving as spare.

## Statistical Terms

### EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average)

Statistical technique giving more weight to recent observations. Used for drift detection and trend analysis.

### CUSUM (Cumulative Sum)

Control chart method for detecting small persistent shifts in a process. Useful for identifying gradual drift from plan.

### Variance

Statistical measure of spread. In resource leveling, lower variance indicates more even workload distribution.

## Next Steps

With this vocabulary established:

- Continue to `03-industry-standards.md` for compliance requirements
- Explore `01-problem-definition.md` for problem context
- Study `02-MATHEMATICAL/01-optimization-theory.md` for mathematical concepts

- **Fix:** Make configurable per request

### VAL-001: Priority Dropdown Ignored

- **Location:** `apps/mms/app/routes/what-if.tsx:54,162-172`
- **Problem:** User selects priority but value is never used
- **Risk:** Client tries it, sees no effect, loses trust
- **Fix:** Pass to solver OR remove from UI

### VAL-002: Scenario Name Not Saved

- **Location:** `apps/mms/app/routes/what-if.tsx:130-135`
- **Problem:** User enters name but it's not persisted
- **Risk:** False expectation of saving
- **Fix:** Save to database OR remove field

### VAL-003: No Business Rule Validation

- **Location:** `packages/api/src/routes/solver.ts:57-105`
- **Problem:** Schema validates structure, not business logic
- **Risk:** Invalid scenarios accepted (e.g., latestPeriod < earliestPeriod)
- **Fix:** Add business rule validation layer

### VAL-004: No Capacity Feasibility Check

- **Location:** `apps/mms/app/routes/what-if.tsx`
- **Problem:** No warning if capacity is impossible for fleet size
- **Risk:** Solver returns INFEASIBLE, user confused
- **Fix:** Pre-validate and warn user

---

## P2 - Medium (Fix For Production)

### OBS-001: Minimal Logging

- **Location:** `packages/api/src/index.ts:48`
- **Problem:** Only startup message logged
- **Risk:** No visibility into production issues
- **Fix:** Add structured logging (Pino/Winston)

### OBS-002: No Metrics Collection

- **Location:** Entire project
- **Problem:** No Prometheus/DataDog integration
- **Risk:** Cannot monitor performance, errors
- **Fix:** Add metrics middleware

### OBS-003: No Audit Trail

- **Location:** Entire project
- **Problem:** No record of who ran what scenarios
- **Risk:** Compliance issues, no debugging history
- **Fix:** Log all operations with user/timestamp

### OBS-004: stderr Only Console.warn

- **Location:** `packages/api/src/adapter/solver-adapter.ts:114`
- **Problem:** HiGHS warnings go to console only
- **Risk:** Lost in production, can't diagnose issues
- **Fix:** Send to logging service (FIXED - but needs production logging)

### PERF-001: 15MB Payload

- **Location:** `apps/mms/app/routes/what-if.tsx` → API
- **Problem:** Frontend generates all data, sends to solver
- **Risk:** Slow, bandwidth waste, poor UX
- **Fix:** Server generates events from DB, frontend sends only parameters

### PERF-002: Subprocess Per Request

- **Location:** `packages/api/src/adapter/solver-adapter.ts`
- **Problem:** New Python process spawned for each solve
- **Risk:** Slow, resource intensive
- **Fix:** Consider long-running solver service or connection pool

### PERF-003: No Caching

- **Location:** Entire project
- **Problem:** Same scenario re-solves every time
- **Risk:** Wasted computation
- **Fix:** Cache results by input hash

### PERF-004: Health Check Spawns Solver

- **Location:** `packages/api/src/routes/solver.ts:173-200`
- **Problem:** Health check runs actual solve (empty problem)
- **Risk:** Slow health checks, unnecessary load
- **Fix:** Simple ping without subprocess

---

## P3 - Low (Fix When Time Permits)

### UI-001: Export Button Placeholder

- **Location:** `apps/mms/app/routes/what-if.tsx:280`
- **Problem:** "Export Results" button does nothing
- **Risk:** Client clicks, nothing happens
- **Fix:** Implement PDF/Excel export OR hide button

### UI-002: Compare Button Placeholder

- **Location:** `apps/mms/app/routes/what-if.tsx:281`
- **Problem:** "Compare Scenarios" button does nothing
- **Risk:** Client clicks, nothing happens
- **Fix:** Implement comparison view OR hide button

### UI-003: Apply Button Placeholder

- **Location:** `apps/mms/app/routes/what-if.tsx:282`
- **Problem:** "Apply to Schedule" button does nothing
- **Risk:** Client clicks, nothing happens
- **Fix:** Implement schedule application OR hide button

### FEAT-001: No PDF/Excel Reports

- **Location:** Not implemented
- **Problem:** RFQ requires report generation
- **Risk:** Missing RFQ requirement
- **Fix:** Phase 2 implementation

### FEAT-002: No Email Notifications

- **Location:** Not implemented
- **Problem:** RFQ requires AlertEngine with email
- **Risk:** Missing RFQ requirement
- **Fix:** Phase 3 implementation

### FEAT-003: No Maximo Integration

- **Location:** Not implemented
- **Problem:** RFQ requires IBM Maximo adapter
- **Risk:** Missing RFQ requirement
- **Fix:** Phase 3 implementation

### FEAT-004: No Role-Based Access

- **Location:** Not implemented
- **Problem:** Everyone has full access
- **Risk:** Security, compliance
- **Fix:** Phase 3 implementation

---

## Resolution Plan

### This Week (Demo)

1. **DATA-001:** Implement MongoDB with document-client
2. **HARD-001/002/003:** Fetch real data from DB
3. **VAL-001:** Either connect Priority dropdown OR remove it
4. **UI-001/002/003:** Hide or disable placeholder buttons
5. **PERF-001:** Refactor to server-side event generation

### Phase 1 (Post-Demo)

1. **SEC-001/002/003:** Full authentication implementation
2. **DATA-002/003:** Persistent rate limiting and scenarios
3. **HARD-004/005/006:** Environment configuration
4. **VAL-002/003/004:** Full validation layer

### Phase 2-3

1. All OBS-\* items (observability)
2. Remaining PERF-\* items
3. All FEAT-\* items

---

## Tracking

| ID       | Status      | Resolved Date | Notes                     |
| -------- | ----------- | ------------- | ------------------------- |
| SEC-001  | OPEN        |               |                           |
| SEC-002  | OPEN        |               |                           |
| SEC-003  | OPEN        |               |                           |
| DATA-001 | IN PROGRESS |               | MongoDB migration planned |
| ...      | ...         | ...           | ...                       |

content.content:
1→# Railway Rolling Stock Maintenance Scheduling: Research & Industry Practices
     2→
     3→> Comprehensive documentation for RAG system - Dubai Metro MMS Project
     4→> Last updated: December 2024
     5→
     6→## Table of Contents
     7→
     8→1. [Executive Summary](#executive-summary)
     9→2. [Problem Definition](#problem-definition)
    10→3. [Planning Hierarchy](#planning-hierarchy)
    11→4. [Optimization Techniques](#optimization-techniques)
    12→5. [Case Studies by Metro System](#case-studies-by-metro-system)
    13→6. [Technology & Systems](#technology--systems)
    14→7. [Academic Research](#academic-research)
    15→8. [Best Practices](#best-practices)
    16→9. [References](#references)
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Executive Summary
    21→
    22→Railway rolling stock maintenance scheduling is a complex optimization problem that combines:
    23→- **Resource allocation** (man-hours, tracks, depots)
    24→- **Time/mileage constraints** (preventive maintenance intervals)
    25→- **Operational requirements** (train availability, service levels)
    26→- **Cost optimization** (labor, materials, penalties)
    27→
    28→Key findings from our research:
    29→
    30→| Aspect | Industry Standard |
    31→|--------|------------------|
    32→| Planning horizons | Daily → Weekly → Monthly → Annual → Long-term (10+ years) |
    33→| Primary trigger | Mileage-based (evolving to condition-based) |
    34→| Common techniques | MIP, Genetic Algorithms, Large Neighborhood Search, Hybrid CP/MIP |
    35→| Software platforms | IBM Maximo, SAP EAM, Custom optimization engines |
    36→| Performance target | 99.9% train availability (Hong Kong MTR benchmark) |
    37→
    38→---
    39→
    40→## Problem Definition
    41→
    42→### The Core Scheduling Problem
    43→
    44→The railway maintenance scheduling problem involves:
    45→
    46→1. **Fleet Management**: Multiple train types with different maintenance requirements
    47→2. **Maintenance Types**: Varying intervals and durations
    48→   - Daily inspections
    49→   - Monthly checks (~15,000 km)
    50→   - Quarterly services (~45,000 km)
    51→   - Annual overhauls (~180,000 km)
    52→   - Major overhauls (4-6 years)
    53→
    54→3. **Resource Constraints**:
    55→   - Limited maintenance tracks
    56→   - Fixed workforce (man-hours per period)
    57→   - Depot capacity
    58→   - Spare parts availability
    59→
    60→4. **Objectives** (often conflicting):
    61→   - Minimize maintenance costs
    62→   - Maximize train availability
    63→   - Level resource utilization
    64→   - Comply with safety regulations
    65→
    66→### Problem Complexity
    67→
    68→According to research from [Wiley - Optimization of Annual Planned Rail Maintenance](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764):
    69→
    70→> "Using the CPLEX MIP solver, researchers managed to solve only 62% of instances within 3 hours. Kiefer et al. (2018) formulated their problem as an MIP model but quickly found runtime limitations on larger instances, exceeding the 24-hour time limit."
    71→
    72→This demonstrates why heuristic and metaheuristic approaches are essential for real-world applications.
    73→
    74→---
    75→
    76→## Planning Hierarchy
    77→
    78→### Multi-Level Planning Structure
    79→
    80→Based on [JICA Urban Railways Handbook](https://www.jica.go.jp/english/activities/issues/transport/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2025/03/26/handbook_en_01.pdf) and industry practices:
    81→
    82→```
    83→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    84→│                     MASTER PLAN (Annual/Multi-Year)             │
    85→│  - Resource allocation across the contract period               │
    86→│  - Major overhaul scheduling                                    │
    87→│  - Budget planning                                              │
    88→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    89→│                     MONTHLY PLAN                                │
    90→│  - Medium-scale repairs allocation                              │
    91→│  - Workload balancing                                           │
    92→│  - Resource adjustment                                          │
    93→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    94→│                     WEEKLY PLAN                                 │
    95→│  - Detailed work orders                                         │
    96→│  - Staff rostering                                              │
    97→│  - Track allocation                                             │
    98→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    99→│                     DAILY PLAN                                  │
   100→│  - Execution scheduling                                         │
   101→│  - Real-time adjustments                                        │
   102→│  - Emergency response                                           │
   103→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   104→```
   105→
   106→### Tokyo Metro Approach
   107→
   108→From [JICA Rolling Stock Maintenance Manuals](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/12251708_02.pdf):
   109→
   110→> "Tokyo Metro employs an 'Equalizing' approach to secure the required work progress, equalize the maintenance workload, and enable rolling stock maintenance personnel to fulfill their duties."
   111→
   112→Key principles:
   113→- **Even workload distribution** across periods
   114→- **Mileage tracking** for devices requiring km-based maintenance
   115→- **Train allocation** at daily, monthly, and annual levels
   116→
   117→---
   118→
   119→## Optimization Techniques
   120→
   121→### 1. Mixed Integer Programming (MIP)
   122→
   123→**Strengths:**
   124→- Mathematically optimal solutions
   125→- Well-understood theory
   126→- Commercial solvers available (Gurobi, CPLEX)
   127→
   128→**Limitations:**
   129→- Scalability issues with large instances
   130→- Long computation times
   131→- Requires linearization of constraints
   132→
   133→**Example Application - Deutsche Bahn:**
   134→
   135→From [Deutsche Bahn Hypergraph Optimization Case Study](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/inte.2020.1069):
   136→
   137→> "DB attributes annual savings of €74 million, an annual reduction of 34,000 tons of CO2 emissions, and the elimination of 600 coupling operations to the implementation of FEO (Fleet Employment Optimization)."
   138→
   139→### 2. Genetic Algorithms (GA)
   140→
   141→**Strengths:**
   142→- Handles complex, non-linear constraints
   143→- Good for multi-objective optimization
   144→- Scalable to large problems
   145→
   146→**Example Application - Hong Kong MTR:**
   147→
   148→From [CityU AI Projects - MTR Scheduling](https://www.cs.cityu.edu.hk/~hwchun/AIProjects/stories/allocation/mtrcscheduling/):
   149→
   150→> "The scheduling system is based on genetic algorithm and heuristic search, which work together with a rule-based system. The AI system improved maintenance efficiency by 50%, contributing to MTR's 99.9% on-time performance for 5 million daily passengers."
   151→
   152→### 3. Large Neighborhood Search (LNS)
   153→
   154→**Strengths:**
   155→- Effective for large-scale problems
   156→- Combines local search with diversification
   157→- Can escape local optima
   158→
   159→**Example Application - Vienna Tram:**
   160→
   161→From [ScienceDirect - Integrated Railway Planning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0968090X16302340):
   162→
   163→> "Kiefer et al. developed a metaheuristic based on large neighborhood search after MIP exceeded 24-hour time limits. Testing on the Vienna tram network—the largest instance in literature—showed effective results."
   164→
   165→### 4. Hybrid Constraint Programming / MIP
   166→
   167→**Strengths:**
   168→- CP generates feasible solutions quickly
   169→- MIP improves solution quality
   170→- Best of both worlds
   171→
   172→**Example Application - Danish Railways:**
   173→
   174→From [ScienceDirect - Hybrid CP/MIP Framework](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0377221717307646):
   175→
   176→> "Whereas using a general purpose MIP solver is only able to solve instances over a two-week planning horizon, the hybrid framework generates good results for problem instances over an eight-week period."
   177→
   178→### 5. Greedy Metaheuristics
   179→
   180→**Strengths:**
   181→- Fast computation
   182→- Good initial solutions
   183→- Easy to implement
   184→
   185→**Example Application - Netherlands ProRail:**
   186→
   187→From [Wiley - Annual Rail Maintenance Optimization](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764):
   188→
   189→> "A hybrid algorithm using evolution strategy and a greedy algorithm was created and shown to outperform all other algorithms, achieving a two-thirds decrease in costs compared to human scheduler solutions."
   190→
   191→### Technique Comparison Matrix
   192→
   193→| Technique | Problem Size | Solution Quality | Speed | Implementation |
   194→|-----------|--------------|------------------|-------|----------------|
   195→| MIP | Small-Medium | Optimal | Slow | Complex |
   196→| Genetic Algorithm | Large | Good | Medium | Moderate |
   197→| Large Neighborhood Search | Large | Good | Fast | Moderate |
   198→| Hybrid CP/MIP | Medium-Large | Very Good | Medium | Complex |
   199→| Greedy Heuristics | Any | Acceptable | Very Fast | Simple |
   200→
   201→---
   202→
   203→## Case Studies by Metro System
   204→
   205→### 1. Metrô São Paulo (In-House Innovation)
   206→*   **Strategy**: **Build (Internal)**.
   207→*   **System**: **SMA (Sistema de Monitoramento de Ativos)**.
   208→*   **Key Feature**: Developed entirely by internal engineering teams. Focuses on "Maintenance 5.0" with heavy use of IoT sensors for predictive failure detection.
   209→*   **Why**: To avoid vendor lock-in and ensure the system fits their specific operational reality perfectly.
   210→
   211→### 2. London Underground (The "Big ERP" Approach)
   212→*   **Strategy**: **Buy (Commercial EAM)**.
   213→*   **System**: **IBM Maximo**.
   214→*   **Key Feature**: Consolidated 60+ legacy systems into one massive Maximo instance.
   215→*   **Limitation**: While great for data storage and workflow, they still struggle with the complex *optimization* of schedules, often requiring custom add-ons or manual intervention for the "hard math".
   216→
   217→### 3. Tokyo Metro (Vendor Integrated)
   218→*   **Strategy**: **Vendor Ecosystem**.
   219→*   **System**: Hitachi **REFMa** / Toshiba Systems.
   220→*   **Key Feature**: Tight integration where the train manufacturer provides the maintenance software. The "Equalizing" methodology is strict, leveling workload across the year to ensure constant workforce utilization.
   221→
   222→### 4. Paris Métro - RATP (Commercialized Internal Tool)
   223→*   **Strategy**: **Build -> Sell**.
   224→*   **System**: **Maint'Up**.
   225→*   **Key Feature**: RATP Dev developed their own SaaS solution to manage their assets and now uses it as a selling point when bidding for operations contracts globally.
   226→
   227→### 5. Hong Kong MTR (AI Pioneer)
   228→*   **Strategy**: **Hybrid (ERP + AI)**.
   229→*   **System**: **IBM Maximo + Custom AI Engine**.
   230→*   **Key Feature**: Uses Maximo for the database but built a famous "Genetic Algorithm" engine (with City University of HK) to handle the actual scheduling. This AI engine is credited with their 99.9% reliability.
   231→
   232→### 6. Dubai Metro (RTA / Keolis MHI)
   233→
   234→**Current State:**
   235→- 128 trains (79 Alstom + 49 Kinki Sharyo)
   236→- Manual mileage management via Excel
   237→- IBM Maximo for asset management (not mileage-specific)
   238→
   239→**Recent Innovations:**
   240→
   241→From [RTA ARIIS System Launch](https://www.mediaoffice.ae/en/news/2025/june/12-06/rta-rolls-out-the-automated-rail-infrastructure-inspection-system-in-dubai-metro):
   242→
   243→> "The Automated Rail Infrastructure Inspection System (ARIIS) is expected to enhance safety by reducing manual inspections by up to 70%, improving infrastructure condition assessment by 40%, and reducing inspection times by 75%."
   244→
   245→From [Parsons RIMMS Project](https://www.parsons.com/project/rail-infrastructure-maintenance-management-system-rimms-dubai-united-arab-emirates-uae/):
   246→
   247→> "RIMMS helps the client move from a reactive approach to maintenance to a proactive, integrated approach informed by data."
   248→
   249→### 2. Tokyo Metro / JR East (Japan)
   250→
   251→**Key Features:**
   252→- ATOS (Autonomous Transport Operation System)
   253→- Integrated control center
   254→- Equalizing maintenance approach
   255→
   256→From [Hitachi ATOS Case Study](https://social-innovation.hitachi.en-us/case_studies/atos/):
   257→
   258→> "JR East implements an incredibly congested schedule of up to 10,000 trains per day. ATOS not only streamlines transport operation control work but also manages maintenance work procedures."
   259→
   260→**Maintenance Philosophy:**
   261→- Even workload distribution
   262→- Mileage-based tracking (wheel grinding, brakes)
   263→- Fixed annual allocation across periods
   264→
   265→### 3. London Underground (TfL)
   266→
   267→**Current Approach:**
   268→- IBM Maximo for 10,000+ technicians
   269→- Predictive maintenance using data analytics
   270→- Condition-based monitoring transition
   271→
   272→From [Computerworld - TfL Predictive Analytics](https://www.computerworld.com/article/1655414/how-tfl-is-using-predictive-analytics-to-keep-the-underground-moving.html):
   273→
   274→> "A project currently in production on the Central line analyzes events to predict when a motor is soon to fail. The expected savings from this is approximately £3 million per year."
   275→
   276→**Key Statistics:**
   277→- 59% of TfL's budget goes to asset maintenance
   278→- 50% of delays caused by asset problems
   279→- £300 million potential savings over train lifecycle
   280→
   281→From [TfL Asset Management Strategy](https://content.tfl.gov.uk/lu-asset-management-strategy.pdf):
   282→
   283→> "Maintenance regimes will be optimized to transfer maintenance periodicities from a calendar/service day based approach to a condition and usage basis."
   284→
   285→### 4. Deutsche Bahn (Germany)
   286→
   287→**Key Innovations:**
   288→
   289→From [KONUX Partnership](https://www.konux.com/konux-receives-another-significant-order-from-deutsche-bahn/):
   290→
   291→> "Deutsche Bahn has achieved a cost reduction of 25% through minimization of downtime and maximization of performance using predictive maintenance."
   292→
   293→**DIANA Platform:**
   294→- 40,000+ turnout drive sensors connected
   295→- Real-time condition monitoring
   296→- Predictive maintenance scheduling
   297→
   298→**FEO System (Fleet Employment Optimization):**
   299→- Hypergraph-based optimization
   300→- €74 million annual savings
   301→- 34,000 tons CO2 reduction
   302→- Collaboration with Zuse Institute Berlin (11 years)
   303→
   304→### 5. SNCF (France)
   305→
   306→**Scale:**
   307→- 1,100 CBM-enabled (Condition-Based Maintenance) trainsets
   308→- 2,500 equipped for remote diagnostics
   309→- 8,000+ variables per train analyzed
   310→- 2,000 real-time variables
   311→
   312→From [SNCF Predictive Maintenance](https://www.sncf.com/en/network-expertise/rolling-stock-division/how-predictive-maintenance-improves-rail-travel):
   313→
   314→> "After over 8 years of development, predictive maintenance is now a reality at SNCF. SNCF is a global leader in predictive maintenance for rail."
   315→
   316→**Academic Research:**
   317→
   318→From [Two-phase heuristic for SNCF](https://hal.science/hal-01936483v1/document):
   319→
   320→> "The first phase solves a train assignment problem by combining a greedy heuristic procedure with integer programming. The second phase schedules train movements while minimizing cancelled arrivals and departures."
   321→
   322→### 6. Netherlands Railways (NS) / ProRail
   323→
   324→**Unique Approach:**
   325→
   326→From [The Netherlands Track Maintenance Safety](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/220249901_The_Netherlands_Schedules_Track_Maintenance_to_Improve_Track_Workers'_Safety):
   327→
   328→> "Researchers developed a two-step method of constructing a four-week maintenance schedule in which each working zone of the main lines is closed to trains at night exactly once. Such a schedule is unique in Europe."
   329→
   330→**Key Statistics:**
   331→- 7,000+ km of railway track
   332→- 165 million kilometers driven by passenger trains annually
   333→- 21 maintenance areas with dedicated contractors
   334→
   335→**Research Contribution:**
   336→
   337→From [Operations Research at Netherlands Railways](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43069-024-00342-8):
   338→
   339→> "A joint project with ProRail developed an optimization approach for adjusting the Basic Days timetable up to 2 months before operations due to planned maintenance works."
   340→
   341→### 7. Singapore MRT (SMRT)
   342→
   343→**Key Features:**
   344→- 83 km, 48 stations
   345→- 77 trains peak / 41 off-peak
   346→- Six depots including Bishan (central), Changi, Ulu Pandan
   347→
   348→From [SMRT Train Operator Scheduling](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1023/A:1016002910561):
   349→
   350→> "An optimization-based approach using bipartite matching for night duties and tabu search for day duties automates train-operator scheduling at SMRT."
   351→
   352→**Depot 4.0 Modernization:**
   353→
   354→From [SMRT Depot 4.0](https://www.smrt.com.sg/news-publications/newsroom/media-releases/from-strength-to-strength-building-a-resilient-future/):
   355→
   356→> "SMRT's Depot 4.0 at Bishan will double train overhaul capacity from two to four trains per month, setting a benchmark for future depot design."
   357→
   358→### 8. Hong Kong MTR
   359→
   360→**World-Class Performance:**
   361→- 10 subway lines, 152 stations
   362→- 5 million passengers daily
   363→- 99.9% on-time performance
   364→- 2,600 engineering works weekly
   365→- 10,000 maintenance personnel
   366→
   367→From [AAAI Award-Winning AI System](https://cdn.aaai.org/AAAI/2005/IAAI05-003.pdf):
   368→
   369→> "The AI Engine improved maintenance efficiency by 50%, allowing more works to be performed within the limited 4-5 hour nightly maintenance window."
   370→
   371→**Awards:**
   372→- 2014 AAAI Innovative Applications Award (Deployed Applications)
   373→- 2005 APICTA Best Industrial Application Award
   374→
   375→---
   376→
   377→## Technology & Systems
   378→
   379→### Enterprise Asset Management (EAM)
   380→
   381→#### IBM Maximo
   382→
   383→From [IBM Maximo for TfL](https://www.ibm.com/case-studies/transport-for-london):
   384→
   385→> "Transport for London keeps the public moving safely, reliably and sustainably when it centralizes its maintenance efforts on IBM Maximo software."
   386→
   387→**Key Capabilities:**
   388→- Work order management
   389→- Asset lifecycle tracking
   390→- Integration with IoT sensors
   391→- Predictive maintenance analytics
   392→- Mobile workforce management
   393→
   394→**Market Position:**
   395→- ~35 years in market
   396→- Industry-specific modules (Transportation, Rail, Aviation)
   397→- Native SAP/Oracle integration
   398→
   399→#### SAP EAM
   400→
   401→**Strengths:**
   402→- Tight ERP integration
   403→- Financial/procurement alignment
   404→- S/4HANA platform
   405→
   406→**Comparison:**
   407→
   408→From [SelectHub - IBM Maximo vs SAP](https://www.selecthub.com/eam/ibm-maximo-and-sap/):
   409→
   410→> "IBM Maximo has deep maintenance functions and industry-specific functionality for various verticals. SAP EAM is deeply embedded within the SAP ERP ecosystem and preferred for seamless financial integration."
   411→
   412→### Predictive Maintenance Technologies
   413→
   414→#### Sensors and IoT
   415→
   416→- **Temperature sensors**: Motor, bearing monitoring
   417→- **Vibration sensors**: Wheel, axle condition
   418→- **Pressure sensors**: Brake system health
   419→- **LiDAR**: Track geometry inspection
   420→- **Cameras**: Visual inspection automation
   421→
   422→#### AI/ML Approaches
   423→
   424→From [TfL Predictive Maintenance](https://techinsight.net/2022/04/predictive-maintenance-london-underground/):
   425→
   426→> "AI helps schedule maintenance during non-peak hours, minimizing disruptions to passenger services by predicting when components will fail."
   427→
   428→### Optimization Solvers
   429→
   430→| Solver | Type | Strengths | Used By |
   431→|--------|------|-----------|---------|
   432→| Gurobi | MIP | Fast, scalable | Deutsche Bahn |
   433→| CPLEX | MIP | Industry standard | Academic research |
   434→| CP Optimizer | CP | Scheduling-specific | Danish Railways |
   435→| OR-Tools | Open source | Flexible | Various |
   436→| Custom GA | Metaheuristic | Problem-specific | Hong Kong MTR |
   437→
   438→---
   439→
   440→## Academic Research
   441→
   442→### Key Papers
   443→
   444→1. **Optimization of annual planned rail maintenance** (2022)
   445→   - Authors: Oudshoorn et al.
   446→   - Journal: Computer-Aided Civil and Infrastructure Engineering
   447→   - [Link](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764)
   448→   - Key finding: Hybrid evolution strategy + greedy outperforms all other approaches
   449→
   450→2. **An optimization model for integrated planning of railway traffic and network maintenance** (2016)
   451→   - Journal: Transportation Research Part C
   452→   - [Link](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0968090X16302340)
   453→   - Key finding: Weekly problems solvable near-optimally within 1 hour
   454→
   455→3. **Deutsche Bahn Schedules Train Rotations Using Hypergraph Optimization** (2020)
   456→   - Journal: INFORMS Journal on Applied Analytics
   457→   - [Link](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/inte.2020.1069)
   458→   - Key finding: €74M annual savings achieved
   459→

   461→   - Conference: AAAI
   462→   - [Link](https://cdn.aaai.org/AAAI/2005/IAAI05-003.pdf)
   463→   - Key finding: 50% improvement in maintenance efficiency
   464→

   466→   - Journal: Operations Research Forum
   467→   - [Link](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43069-024-00342-8)
   468→   - Key finding: Comprehensive OR applications overview
   469→
   470→6. **Collaborative optimization of depot location, capacity and rolling stock scheduling** (2024)
   471→   - Journal: Scientific Reports
   472→   - [Link](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-57902-5)
   473→   - Key finding: Integrated depot and scheduling optimization
   474→
   475→7. **Operations Research Models for Scheduling Railway Infrastructure Maintenance** (PhD Thesis)
   476→   - Author: Gabriella Budai-Balke
   477→   - Institution: Erasmus University Rotterdam
   478→   - [Link](https://repub.eur.nl/pub/16008/thesis_GabriellaBudaiBalke_TI456.pdf)
   479→   - Key finding: Genetic and memetic algorithms for maintenance scheduling
   480→
   481→### Research Institutions
   482→
   483→- **Zuse Institute Berlin (ZIB)**: 11-year collaboration with Deutsche Bahn
   484→- **Erasmus University Rotterdam**: Railway maintenance optimization
   485→- **TU Delft**: Dutch railway research
   486→- **City University of Hong Kong**: MTR AI scheduling
   487→
   488→---
   489→
   490→## Best Practices
   491→
   492→### 1. Maintenance Trigger Selection
   493→
   494→| Trigger Type | Best For | Limitations |
   495→|--------------|----------|-------------|
   496→| Time-based | Simple assets | Over/under maintenance |
   497→| Mileage-based | Wear-dependent items | Tracking complexity |
   498→| Condition-based | Critical components | Sensor investment |
   499→| Hybrid | Most applications | Implementation complexity |
   500→
   501→From [Railway Technical Website](http://www.railway-technical.com/trains/train-maintenance/):
   502→
   503→> "Rolling stock maintenance can be programmed by mileage, by time, or by condition monitoring. Condition monitoring is most recent and reliable, relying on automated data collection and sensors."
   504→
   505→### 2. Resource Leveling
   506→
   507→**Goal**: Even distribution of workload to avoid peaks and valleys
   508→
   509→**Techniques**:
   510→- Move flexible events within tolerance windows
   511→- Balance by fleet type
   512→- Consider depot-specific constraints
   513→- Account for seasonal variations
   514→
   515→From Tokyo Metro practice:
   516→
   517→> "Allocating an even workload in the annual maintenance plan provides advantages for both resource utilization and service reliability."
   518→
   519→### 3. Mileage Equalization
   520→
   521→**Problem**: Uneven mileage leads to clustered maintenance
   522→
   523→From [ScienceDirect - Long-term Fleet Management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2210970623000628):
   524→
   525→> "A sub-optimal choice of wagons can lead to situations requiring complex shunting operations. If wagons were clustered by mileage, the process would be greatly simplified."
   526→
   527→**Solution**: Strategic run assignment to balance fleet mileage
   528→
   529→### 4. Integration with Operations
   530→
   531→**Key Principle**: Maintenance and operations must be coordinated
   532→
   533→From [KONUX and Deutsche Bahn](https://www.konux.com/konux-receives-another-significant-order-from-deutsche-bahn/):
   534→
   535→> "Real-time insights into current and future condition of infrastructure enables scheduling timely interventions such as grinding, welding, or replacement."
   536→
   537→### 5. Performance Metrics
   538→
   539→| Metric | Definition | Target |
   540→|--------|------------|--------|
   541→| MTBF | Mean Time Between Failures | Maximize |
   542→| MDBF | Mean Distance Between Failures | Maximize |
   543→| Availability | % time train is operational | >99% |
   544→| Utilization | % capacity used | 70-90% |
   545→| Compliance | % on-time maintenance | >95% |
   546→
   547→---
   548→
   549→## References
   550→
   551→### Official Documentation & Reports
   552→
   553→1. [JICA Operations and Maintenance of Urban Railways Handbook](https://www.jica.go.jp/english/activities/issues/transport/__icsFiles/afieldfile/2025/03/26/handbook_en_01.pdf)
   554→2. [JICA Rolling Stock Maintenance Manuals (Appendix)](https://openjicareport.jica.go.jp/pdf/12251708_02.pdf)
   555→3. [TfL Asset Management Strategy](https://content.tfl.gov.uk/lu-asset-management-strategy.pdf)
   556→4. [Gurobi Deutsche Bahn Case Study](https://assets.gurobi.com/pdfs/case-study-DB-English-low-res.pdf)
   557→
   558→### News & Industry Sources
   559→
   560→5. [RTA ARIIS Launch](https://www.mediaoffice.ae/en/news/2025/june/12-06/rta-rolls-out-the-automated-rail-infrastructure-inspection-system-in-dubai-metro)
   561→6. [SNCF Predictive Maintenance](https://www.sncf.com/en/network-expertise/rolling-stock-division/how-predictive-maintenance-improves-rail-travel)
   562→7. [TfL Predictive Analytics](https://www.computerworld.com/article/1655414/how-tfl-is-using-predictive-analytics-to-keep-the-underground-moving.html)
   563→8. [Deutsche Bahn AI for Maintenance](https://www.bestpractice.ai/ai-case-study-best-practice/deutsche_bahn_reduces_maintenance_cost_by_25%25_and_delay-causing_failures_using_machine_learning)
   564→
   565→### Academic Papers
   566→
   567→9. [Optimization of annual planned rail maintenance (Wiley)](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/mice.12764)
   568→10. [Integrated railway traffic and maintenance planning (ScienceDirect)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0968090X16302340)
   569→11. [DB Hypergraph Optimization (INFORMS)](https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/inte.2020.1069)
   570→12. [MTR Engineering Works Scheduling (AAAI)](https://cdn.aaai.org/AAAI/2005/IAAI05-003.pdf)
   571→13. [Hybrid CP/MIP Framework (ScienceDirect)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0377221717307646)
   572→14. [Operations Research at NS (Springer)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43069-024-00342-8)
   573→15. [Singapore MRT Scheduling (Springer)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1023/A:1016002910561)
   574→16. [Depot and Rolling Stock Optimization (Nature)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-024-57902-5)
   575→
   576→### Technology Vendors
   577→
   578→17. [IBM Maximo](https://www.ibm.com/products/maximo)
   579→18. [Parsons RIMMS Dubai](https://www.parsons.com/project/rail-infrastructure-maintenance-management-system-rimms-dubai-united-arab-emirates-uae/)
   580→19. [KONUX Predictive Maintenance](https://www.konux.com/)
   581→20. [Hitachi ATOS](https://social-innovation.hitachi/en-us/case_studies/atos/)
   582→
   583→### Research Institutions
   584→
   585→21. [CityU Hong Kong AI Projects](https://www.cs.cityu.edu.hk/~hwchun/AIProjects/)
   586→22. [Erasmus University Rotterdam Thesis Repository](https://repub.eur.nl/pub/16008/)
   587→23. [TU Delft Transport Planning](https://www.tudelft.nl/en/ceg/about-faculty/departments/transport-planning/)
   588→
   589→---
   590→
   591→## Appendix: Glossary
   592→
   593→| Term | Definition |
   594→|------|------------|
   595→| **CBM** | Condition-Based Maintenance |
   596→| **CP** | Constraint Programming |
   597→| **EAM** | Enterprise Asset Management |
